title: Future of Warfare: Drones, Distributed Sensors, and the AI Advantage
content:
A war's success will depend in large part on whether the military adopts new technologies. The old maxim that quantity has a quality of its own holds true for technological breakthroughs. Cheap and abundant aerial drones, uncrewed surface vehicles, and distributed sensors will be crucial. High-end capabilities and legacy systems remain vital as well. However, new operational concepts and updated frameworks for command and control are also necessary.

The military cannot fight the wars of the future with battle plans from the past. The Biden administration's National Defense Strategy established integrated deterrence to encompass new technological domains. This diffusion ensures that allied forces retain the ability to sense, maneuver, and strike under heavy attack. Competition is not inconsistent with cooperation in this modern landscape. Integrating these capabilities across partner forces is essential for maintaining a strategic edge.

Artificial intelligence is central to these operational shifts. AI-enabled systems already play a growing role in optimizing logistics and analyzing intelligence. Over time, these systems will increasingly shape how militaries train, plan, and fight. China is already integrating AI capabilities to bypass traditional U.S. advantages. Deploying AI effectively will require cultural and organizational changes, including new approaches to procurement.

It is vital that policy frameworks prioritize the responsible adoption of AI. The debate over using large language models has revealed that norms have not caught up with capabilities. A consensus grounded in law and values must be hammered out to ensure ethical AI use. Doing so will make competing in military innovation more challenging, since rivals may not follow the same rules. But it is nonetheless essential for democratic nations to uphold these principles.

Effective military innovation cannot rest on individual capabilities alone. It must be built on networked defense, integrating advanced technologies across allied militaries. Partnerships such as AUKUS illustrate what is possible in technology transfer. This arrangement acts as a technology accelerator for quantum navigation and undersea warfare. Similarly, NATO has launched its own defense innovation accelerator to foster collective advancement.
